item.title: LLMs & Agents: How do they Work?
item.description: For as much as AI has dramatically changed all of our lives, mine included, I was a bit uneasy that I didn’t really know how they are implemented and how they work. What is thinking and chain-of-thought reasoning? How does it choose a tool? What are parameters, exactly, and why are more of them better? What’s an Agent? What’s MCP?

item.title: The founder box
item.description: Thinking inside boxes

item.title: Breaking Up With WordPress After Two Decades | Yusuf Aytas
item.description: Moving off WordPress was not really about cost or performance. It was about turning years of writing into a searchable, linkable archive instead of leaving it as a pile of published pages.

item.title: I made an MVP in one day - Diego López
item.description: Hello. It all starts with my pressing need to have a Cornhole. Do you know that feeling when you know you don’t need something, but you need it? Well, that’s how I was, doing deep research all over the web, comparing prices, studying second-hand markets, looking at how to build one myself… Talking to Chinese […]

item.title: Good Bonds | Robot Daycare
item.description: Engineering contraptions

item.title: Moving my mobile numbers to VoIP
item.description: Porting my personal and work mobile numbers to AAISP, using Groundwire as the client, and a small self-hosted proxy to plug the SMS gap.
item.author: (Danny)

item.title: The Economics of Forced Mitigation
item.description: The difficult part of the Mythos discussion is not that a frontier model became better at finding and exploiting vulnerabilities. That was always going to happen. The harder part is that the surrounding response already assumes continued capability growth, continued diffusion, and continued dependence, while the burden of coping with those conditions is pushed onto the organizations expected to defend themselves inside them. This is not just a one-off model release. It is an emerging pattern of forced mitigation. This analysis is a projection grounded in current evidence. The capital buildout, capability gains, selective access, and compression of offensive labor are observable today. The broader effects discussed here follow as a likely trajectory under those conditions, but they are not yet uniform across all environments. My own position is not neutral. I think the arrangement now taking shape is morally wrong, and the structure is enough to show why. A concentrated set of actors is building the capability, capturing the gains, normalizing the dependency, and pushing much of the operational burden outward. That judgment does not change the immediate condition. The fire has already started. Most organizations are no longer deciding whether to live near it. They are deciding how not to burn. A Familiar Pattern Security discussions tend to follow a familiar pattern. A new capability appears, it is framed as a step change, and urgency follows, with attackers expected to move faster, defenders assumed to fall behind, and organizations told that everything must change. The Mythos narrative fits that pattern closely. The Cloud Security Alliance says AI has materially accelerated vulnerability discovery beyond current defensive operating capacity and that immediate operational changes are required (CSA MythosReady, CSA CISO Briefing). That is a fair reading of the operational problem, but it is still incomplete. The harder part is that the model builder itself is also documenting uncertainty, late-discovered evaluation gaps, and problematic behavior in systems it chose not to release broadly (Anthropic System Card). The same moment being presented as a step change in attacker capability is also a moment in which the producer of that capability is admitting that capability, evaluation, and control are not moving in lockstep. There is still no public evidence showing that APT groups or criminal operators are running fully autonomous, end-to-end AI-driven attack pipelines at scale, and that distinction matters because too much of the discussion tries to jump from benchmark performance to operational certainty. The CSA itself does not make that jump. Its own offensive security framing says AI augments offensive workflows rather than replacing operators (CSA AI Offensive Security Report). Anthropic’s system card stays within the same boundary even while describing a “striking leap in cyber capabilities” and the ability to autonomously discover and exploit zero-day vulnerabilities in major operating systems and browsers in controlled testing (Anthropic System Card). Full autonomy in the field is still the wrong claim to make. Scale has moved far enough to change the operating conditions anyway. The vendor-centered shape of the discussion matters too. Once capability is framed as uniquely dangerous, the supplier also becomes the actor defining access, explaining risk, and presenting itself as the party responsible for governing misuse. Microsoft’s February 2025 announcement about disrupting a cybercrime network abusing generative AI shows the same pattern from another angle: platform control, threat communication, and market positioning become entangled once AI capability is treated as both strategic infrastructure and a managed risk layer (Microsoft). The distinction matters. Where stolen credentials, proxying, or safeguard evasion are involved, “abuse” is the right term because the access and control path itself has been violated. Where a permissive model is simply used as delivered for harmful ends, the better term is malicious use. Amplification Over Autonomy AI reduces the cost of vulnerability research and exploit development, but it does not remove the work. The CSA material shows both sides at once: large-scale vulnerability identification across codebases and assistance in exploit construction, but still with continued reliance on human validation, environmental context, and execution decisions (CSA MythosReady). Anthropic’s system card points in the same direction, because the model is described as highly capable in cyber tasks while still surrounded by a governance layer that treats misuse, concealment, and oversight failures as real concerns rather than solved problems (Anthropic System Card). The capability is real, and so are the constraints. Treating one as proof that the other disappeared is where the discussion starts going soft. The AISI evaluation helps because it is concrete. It does not present Mythos as a better chatbot with security knowledge, but as a model that, in controlled evaluations with network access and explicit tasking, could execute multi-stage attacks on vulnerable networks and discover and exploit vulnerabilities autonomously, while also becoming the first model to complete their 32-step “The Last Ones” corporate attack simulation from start to finish in some runs and averaging 22 of 32 steps overall (AISI evaluation). AISI also states plainly that this does not establish robust real-world autonomy against defended enterprise environments. That limitation matters. It narrows the claim. It does not undo the result. Frontier-model cyber capability is no longer merely assistive in any ordinary sense, even if it is not yet a drop-in replacement for skilled operators across real defensive environments. The harder question is what follows when the cost of producing “good enough” results drops sharply. That is the threshold that counts. Defenders do not need a world full of autonomous expert attackers to face structural pressure. They only need a larger population of actors who can now produce operationally relevant results. If the cost of producing usable vulnerability research, exploit scaffolding, or chained attack steps decreases, then more actors cross the minimum threshold required to produce results that matter operationally, even if their average sophistication does not change much. Security operations were built around a smaller and slower adversary population. Once that assumption breaks, the system comes under pressure because many more actors are now competent enough. That pressure is not theoretical. Public indicators already point to faster discovery load and shorter exploitation windows, which means the threshold for causing real defensive strain is falling even without full attacker autonomy. Real-world reporting also points in that direction. Dark Reading’s coverage of TeamPCP describes a cloud-focused campaign that compromised at least 60,000 servers through worm-like propagation, broad scanning of exposed services, and large-scale automation of well-known techniques rather than novel tradecraft (Dark Reading). That is not evidence of frontier-model deployment at scale. It is evidence that industrialized offensive pressure is already sufficient to create major strain without anything like full autonomy. Time Was a Control Security systems have long relied on time as one of several implicit controls. Segmentation, privilege boundaries, exploit mitigations, and detection engineering all matter, but triage, reproduction, prioritization, patching, escalation, and coordination also rely on delay, and large parts of operational security were built on the assumption that this delay existed and could be managed. The CSA says AI-driven discovery is now outpacing remediation capacity (CSA CISO Briefing), while external threat intelligence points in the same direction, with timelines between disclosure and exploitation continuing to shrink (Google Threat Intelligence, CISA KEV). Time was never the only control, but many organizations depended on it more than they admitted. That dependence is becoming harder to hide. Public threat intelligence has already shown median time-to-exploit compressing sharply over time, from roughly 63 days in 2018–2019 to 32 days in 2022 and as low as five days in 2023. The compression trend predates Mythos, but frontier-model capability is now plausibly accelerating a pressure that was already visible. The AISI results deepen that point because they are not limited to post-disclosure opportunism. Their range work points to compression of offensive labor itself. Tasks that would take human professionals days of work were completed inside model-driven attack sequences, and their “The Last Ones” range is described as a 32-step corporate network attack simulation estimated to require around 20 hours of human effort (AISI evaluation). This still does not prove that temporal compression is the dominant control everywhere, because other controls remain relevant and in some environments may remain decisive, but it shows something simpler and harder to dismiss: the time available for remediation is shrinking relative to exposure, and that alone creates strain. Once that buffer compresses, the system starts behaving differently. The limiting factor shifts from discovery to response capacity. In a lot of environments, delay did not merely buy time; it masked weaknesses. It hid poor ownership, slow prioritization, overloaded teams, weak asset knowledge, and the long administrative latency between identification and action. When delay compresses, those weaknesses stop looking like management annoyances and start showing up as operational failures. The Economics and Politics That Produce the Risk The CSA describes the shift as systemic and not tied to a specific model or vendor (CSA CISO Briefing). That matters, but “systemic” can mean several things at once, so it is worth being precise. The problem is technological because the capability itself is broadly useful, market-structural because a relatively small number of actors build and control much of the underlying infrastructure, political because states, regulators, and major institutions are already helping normalize the buildout, and operational because the same models, APIs, tooling patterns, and workflows are spreading across development and security practice. A more direct explanation is capital, cost, and timing. Large-scale investment in models, compute, and datacenters reduces the cost of certain forms of cognitive labor, including vulnerability discovery, exploit development, code review, and adjacent security work. That by itself increases output. But the timing matters too. The infrastructure supporting the buildout is financed and depreciated on long horizons, while the capabilities running on top of it turn over much faster. Datacenters and facilities are committed for years. Model generations and AI hardware cycles move much faster. That mismatch does not resolve the contradiction. It defers it. The same buildout that expands capability also expands the burden of managing the risks that capability creates. The point is not that the buildout resolves its tensions. It delays their reckoning by extending the period in which capital can search for revenue, wider deployment, and institutional dependence. The outward push is not only ordinary competition for demand. Expansion into more sectors, workflows, and institutions is not only demand discovery. It is also how already-committed infrastructure finds somewhere to land profitably. It also looks like an effort to generate returns on infrastructure commitments that are already made and cannot be unwound cheaply. The gains from that buildout appear to accrue primarily to a small set of hyperscalers, accelerator suppliers, frontier model labs, and their investors. The buildout is also concentrated. A small number of hyperscalers control most enterprise cloud infrastructure, and NVIDIA currently controls roughly 80–90% of AI accelerator revenue, which makes the buildout dependent not only on a small set of hyperscalers but also on a highly concentrated hardware supply chain. The burden side is no longer just intuition. The divergence is visible in plain terms: CVE volume nearly tripled from 17,305 in 2019 to 48,185 in 2025, while critical-severity remediation timelines remained roughly in the 65–74 day range. Defensive labor capacity is also under pressure. In the United States alone, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reports about 182,800 information security analyst jobs in 2024, with about 16,000 openings projected each year from 2024 to 2034, while CyberSeek reports 514,359 cybersecurity job listings in its latest period. These are converging indicators rather than proof of a single causal model. The exact contribution of AI to each trend is not yet isolated, but the directional problem is still clear enough: discovery and exposure are expanding faster than defensive capacity is improving. The system scales by expanding capability, but the cost of managing the resulting risk does not stay where that capability is produced. It is distributed across those operating on top of it. More capability leads to more usage. More usage leads to more discovery. More discovery increases exposure, and increased exposure turns into more remediation load, more incident handling, more triage pressure, and more demand for defensive tooling and staffing. The full overload condition is not yet uniform, but the direction is clear. That distribution of gains and burdens becomes most visible when state actors enter the picture simultaneously as endorsers of the capability and managers of the risks it creates. In April 2026, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent joined warnings to major banks about the cyber risks of Mythos and, within days, publicly praised the same model as a strategic breakthrough in the U.S. competition with China (Reuters, Bloomberg Law). That is the tension in compact form: the capability is endorsed as an asset at the level of national strategy while the burden of managing its risks is pushed onto downstream institutions told to defend themselves against it. Anthropic’s own framing reflects the same structure at the firm level. The same capability is positioned as useful for defense while also acknowledged as creating offensive risk, with access controlled through limited release and partner coordination rather than through any meaningful technical separation of defensive and offensive potential (Anthropic System Card). That governance is not only a legitimating layer. It is also a stabilizing mechanism meant to keep the buildout politically and institutionally tolerable long enough for expansion to continue. Calling this merely dual-use misses what is actually different here. Cloud infrastructure was dual-use. Open-source security tooling was dual-use. Exploit frameworks were dual-use. What distinguishes this moment is the combination of speed, scale, concentration, and political backing. Capability expands rapidly, cost drops, access broadens, and pressure accumulates downstream. From Avoidance to Forced Mitigation Risk treatment options are straightforward on paper: accept, transfer, avoid, or reduce. The reason “forced mitigation” fits this discussion is that the current buildout does not leave those options equally available. The force here is structural rather than formal: the options still exist on paper, but they no longer exist on anything like equal practical terms. Avoidance is narrowing, transfer is incomplete, acceptance is constrained, and reduction becomes the default response for organizations expected to keep operating inside the system. From a risk management perspective, avoidance would mean limiting or rejecting a capability that introduces technological, market-structural, political, and operational risk. In theory that remains coherent. In practice it is no longer a serious option for most organizations, because the capability is already embedded in development workflows, tooling stacks, vendor ecosystems, procurement assumptions, and competitive expectations. Refusing it often means refusing the surrounding operating model that has formed around it, and that carries operational cost, competitive cost, and increasingly organizational cost as well. This is where the structure matters most: the buildout in compute, models, and infrastructure does not just expand capability, it also reshapes the conditions under which organizations make decisions, until avoidance starts to look less like a strategic option and more like an institutional refusal to operate inside the prevailing system. The CSA report should be read within that constraint. It operates at the defensive layer and assumes continued capability growth, attacker access, and persistent exposure. That is why its recommendations are operationally sensible and structurally limited at the same time. If increased capability produces increased load, then more people, more capacity, more tooling, faster procurement, tighter governance, stronger playbooks, and more automation follow logically. The report says as much. It explicitly prepares organizations for more incidents, more triage, more remediation pressure, more staff strain, and more burnout risk, while also calling for formalized AI usage across security functions, AI-driven review before code ships, and purpose-built agents to help teams operate at adversary speed (CSA MythosReady). None of that is irrational once you accept the constraint. OpenAI’s latest cyber announcement points in the same direction rather than changing the pattern. The company is expanding Trusted Access for Cyber, adding access tiers for verified defenders, and introducing GPT-5.4-Cyber as a more cyber-permissive model for vetted users, while explicitly arguing that defensive access should scale in step with model capability growth (OpenAI). In practice, this follows the same broad pattern as Anthropic’s approach: capability growth is treated as given, access is tiered, and governance is handled through selective release rather than technical separation. Organizations control how they detect, prioritize, and remediate. They do not control how quickly capability spreads, how cheaply it is delivered, or how widely it is used. They are expected to absorb the effects. That is not only a technical asymmetry. It is a distribution of responsibility and risk. The same capability layer increases the pressure, and the proposed answer is deeper integration with that same layer in order to cope with the conditions it is helping create. The recommendation can still be correct, but it also reveals the structure around it. More capability increases pressure. Pressure demands more capacity. More capacity is increasingly defined as more AI. The system answers the consequences of capability expansion with further adoption of the same capability. This does not look like firms choosing freely among equal options. It looks like firms responding to competitive conditions that make outward expansion, selective access, and downstream cost externalization increasingly hard to avoid. Mitigation is not being selected after a menu of equal options. It is being imposed by a system in which avoidance is no longer practical and escalation of capacity is treated as the only serious response. None of that makes the arrangement legitimate. It only makes it real. That structural judgment has immediate operational consequences, and they are what most of this audience will actually be managing. The next question is no longer whether the system is just. It is how to protect people, systems, and institutions inside conditions they did not choose. The managerial implication is straightforward. CISOs and engineering leaders should stop assuming that backlog growth is temporary, that existing approval cycles are compatible with the threat environment, or that AI can be treated as an optional side initiative. They need to assume higher discovery volume, shorter usable response windows, more simultaneous remediation decisions, and more human strain around prioritization and execution. That means clearer ownership, less process drag, faster containment authority, better asset knowledge, and more explicit decisions about what work will not be done. Buying tools faster is not the main adjustment here. The real adjustment is accepting that the old tempo no longer holds. Throughput Over Robustness There is a familiar assumption in these discussions that more speed produces better outcomes. Faster discovery should mean faster fixes, and faster fixes should mean stronger engineering discipline. That is possible. It has not been established. The CSA confirms increased throughput. It does not demonstrate increased quality (CSA AI Offensive Security Report). Anthropic’s system card documents something narrower but still important: rare disallowed actions, apparent obfuscation, and late-discovered evaluation oversights in high-capability systems (Anthropic System Card). Public indicators already point in the wrong direction for anyone assuming that speed automatically improves robustness. Veracode’s 2026 State of Software Security report found that high-severity exploitable flaws rose 36% year over year, while security debt was present in 82% of organizations, up from 74% the year before. That does not prove AI caused the deterioration. It does undercut the claim that faster, more AI-assisted development is already producing cleaner or more controllable outcomes. That is why the quality argument has to remain testable. If the claim is that speed improves robustness, then environments adopting AI-assisted workflows should show it in measurable terms: defect density, regression rates, remediation latency, rework volume, and similar indicators. Until then, speed should not be treated as a proxy for quality. What This Actually Means Discovery is faster, timelines are shorter, exposure is increasing, and operational strain is growing. The mechanisms are not all directly measured in a single model, but the direction is visible enough: reduced cost, concentrated infrastructure, broader access, and a capability stack that is being pushed outward faster than governance and remediation can keep up. More burden lands on defensive teams. More effort is required to maintain the same level of control. More of that effort is framed as adopting the same capability layer that contributed to the pressure. That is enough to treat the issue as technological, market-structural, political, and operational at the same time. Capability growth is expected. Risk growth is tolerated. Mitigation is delegated. This does not look like a breakdown. It looks like a system designed, whether deliberately or not, to keep reproducing the same distribution of gains and burdens across the firms, institutions, and operational practices now arranged around it. The central question is not whether capability is increasing. It is where the cost of that increase lands and who is expected to carry it. In practical terms, the message to CISOs is blunt: the wider order shaping this market is producing a harsher operating environment, and much of the immediate burden of coping with it is being pushed onto the organizations expected to defend themselves. The practical implication is broader than a temporary surge in workload. The CSA’s own framing already points toward a permanent shift in operating assumptions, where AI-driven offense becomes a baseline condition and vulnerability operations become a standing organizational capability rather than a periodic function (CSA CISO Briefing). That means the burden is no longer only to respond faster. It is to reorganize teams, workflows, and authority around a permanently harsher tempo. That broader alignment matters too. The current buildout is being justified through necessity, urgency, and strategic advantage language, and that language helps make a political-economic choice sound like an unavoidable technical response. It helps legitimate the buildout. Capital, infrastructure, and institutional backing are being concentrated around a specific technological order, while the costs of the instability it produces are dispersed much more widely. The contradiction is already visible. The same systems presented as sources of strength are also producing cyber risk, institutional dependence, and operational strain, while the burden of dealing with those effects does not land where the gains do. Whether liability rules, disclosure obligations, or compute-governance regimes could alter that trajectory is a separate question. The narrower point here is simpler: current market practice assumes the trajectory will continue, and what it manages is not a temporary imbalance but a contradiction it keeps displacing rather than resolving. The gains are concentrated in the firms and investors financing and controlling the capability, while the cost of living with its consequences is dispersed across the organizations expected to defend themselves. My own view, and I state it here as opinion rather than fact, is that this is now better understood as a structural shift than a purely technical one, and it is also why I think the arrangement is morally wrong. A small set of actors built the condition, captured the gains, normalized the dependency, and distributed much of the burden outward. The people and organizations now being told to cope with the consequences are mostly not the ones who created them. That is the structure. The rest is response. The conditions that make it necessary were not of their choosing, and none of them can exit alone.
item.author: (Zafer Balkan)

item.title: JIT Compilers and Cache Coherency
item.description: Deep-dive into cache coherency considerations for JIT compilers on AArch64

item.title: How To Stay Sane Running a ‘Default Dead’ Startup
item.description: I’m in my 40s, have a family, kids, and my startup is default dead . But I’m calm and confident that I’ll dig my way out. I haven’t always felt this way. Here’s how I changed that.

item.title: My experience with Claude and Codex on a system architecture bug
item.description: I gave two state-of-the-art AI models the same architecture bug. I was quite surprised with the difference in results.

item.title: I Applied to 1,600 Jobs. That’s Why I Got 4 Offers. — Jelena Dobric
item.description: Two months. 1,600+ applications. 4 offers. At companies I actually liked. Hiring market was not great. Plenty of strong candidates. Plenty of “we’ve decided to move forward with other applicants.” And yet—this worked. Not because I had a perfect resume. Not because I networked my way in. Not becaus

item.title: The Skills That Matter Now
item.description: AI changed what engineers spend their time on. The skills that separate engineers who thrive from those still catching up.

item.title: iPhone (17?) Air, One Month Later
item.description: I fear I am about to feel what iPhone Mini people feel.

item.title: Spec Driven Development isn't Waterfall
item.description: Write down what you mean.

item.title: Postgres: One Database to Rule Them All | Marmot
item.description: I'm a huge fan of simple software. There's something really satisfying about solving a problem and reducing the number of moving parts. Most of the time, you'll find the tool you already have can do more than you thought.
item.author: (https://github.com/charlie-haley)

item.title: The player/coach trap: why Engineering Managers shouldn’t be expected to code
item.description: When managers code, teams lose leadership.

item.title: What Breaks When You Run Coding Agents Unsupervised
item.description: Five failure modes that surface only when coding agents run without anyone watching. None involve code generation.
item.author: (Serghei Iakovlev)

item.title: Bash is the SQL for file systems
item.description: Introducing serverless execution: run bash on Archil file systems directly, without provisioning compute or moving data. Live today in all AWS regions.

item.title: The Case for Semi-Autonomous Nonprofits - Emre Sarbak
item.description: AI can automate 50-70% of nonprofit operations. Unlike businesses, where automation enriches owners, automating a nonprofit sends all the value to the people it serves.
item.author: (Emre Sarbak)

item.title: Posting on the internet freightens me
A Survey of the Ticket (Re)Selling Landscape
item.description: A Survey of the Ticket (Re)Selling Landscape

item.title: How I learned Unity the wrong way
item.description: I spent 3 years building Unity games by copying tutorials and stitching forum scripts together. Then an interviewer asked me what Queue<T> was. I could not a...
item.author: (Darko Tomic)

item.title: Codex for (almost) everything
item.description: The updated Codex app for macOS and Windows adds computer use, in-app browsing, image generation, memory, and plugins to accelerate developer workflows.

item.title: Thunderbolt — AI You Control
item.description: The Open-Source, Cross-Platform, Extensible AI Client

item.title: The Future of Everything is Lies, I Guess: Where Do We Go From Here?
item.description: This is a long article, so I've broken it up into a series of posts, listed below. You can also read the full work as a PDF or EPUB.

item.title: Email for agents - Cloudflare Email Service now in public beta
item.description: Agents are becoming multi-channel. That means making them available wherever your users already are — including the inbox. Today, Cloudflare Email Service enters public beta with the infrastructure layer to make that easy: send, receive, and process email natively from your agents.
item.author: (https://www.facebook.com/https://www.linkedin.com/in/thomas-gauvin/)

item.title: Introducing Claude Opus 4.7
item.description: Anthropic is an AI safety and research company that's working to build reliable, interpretable, and steerable AI systems.

item.title: Qwen Studio
item.description: QWEN STUDIO HUGGING FACE MODELSCOPE DISCORD Following the launch of Qwen3.6-Plus, we are excited to open-source Qwen3.6-35B-A3B — a sparse yet remarkably capable mixture-of-experts (MoE) model with 35 billion total parameters and only 3 billion active parameters. Despite its efficiency, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B delivers outstanding agentic coding performance, surpassing its predecessor Qwen3.5-35B-A3B by a wide margin and rivaling much larger dense models such as Qwen3.5-27B and Gemma4-31B. Still supporting both multimodal thinking and non-thinking modes, Qwen3.

item.title: The API Tooling Crisis: Why developers are abandoning Postman and its clones?
item.description: I’ve been a Postman user for my entire developer career. I remember when it was just a simple Chrome extension that made pentesting APIs slightly less painful. Those were simpler times. Today, I find myself joining the growing exodus of developers abandoning these tools because they sell out.

item.title: Unexpected €54k billing spike in 13 hours: Firebase browser key without API restrictions used for Gemini requests - Gemini API - Google AI Developers Forum
item.description: Hello, We are looking for guidance regarding an unexpected €54,000+ Gemini API charge that occurred within a few hours after enabling Firebase AI Logic on an existing Firebase project. Background: We created the project over a year ago and initially used it only for Firebase Authentication. Recently, we added a simple AI feature (generating a web snippet from a text prompt) and enabled Firebase AI Logic. What happened: Shortly after enabling this, we experienced a sudden and extreme spike i...

item.title: Wargames Movie Magic: Where it Cheats - IMSAI & WOPR!
item.description: Dave takes you through the computers behind the scenes in the movie WarGames! Comments with question marks get answered on our weekly ShopTalk!: https://youtu.be/iT0T52OV9B8
item.author: (Dave's Garage)

item.title: Visualizing Claude Code MCP Requests with Coding Agent Explorer
item.description: See exactly how Claude Code communicates with MCP servers. Use the MCP Observer to monitor requests, debug tool usage, and gain full visibility into coding agent behavior.

item.title: Darkbloom — Private AI Inference on Apple Silicon
item.description: Decentralized inference on hardware-verified Apple Silicon. End-to-end encrypted. The node operator never sees your data. OpenAI-compatible — change one line.

item.title: Friends Don't Let Friends Use Ollama | Sleeping Robots
item.description: Ollama gained traction by being the first easy llama.cpp wrapper, then spent years dodging attribution, misleading users, and pivoting to cloud, all while riding VC money earned on someone else's engine. Here's the full history, and why the alternatives are better.
item.author: (Zetaphor)

item.title: Burgers | マクドナルド公式
item.description: Check out McDonald's new, limited and regular burger menus for breakfast, regular and dinner time. Order and pay in advance on your smartphone without waiting at the cashier and just pick up at store nearby.

item.title: Rome Is Burning
item.description: Three fires in one week. A Molotov cocktail at Sam Altman's mansion, an arson at a toilet paper factory, a mall ablaze. The common thread is not crime. It's what happens when you replace human judgment with spreadsheets.

item.title: Startups are context arbitrages
item.description: If all code will be written by LLMs, build vs buy will simply be a context arbitrage between startups and their customers.

item.title: Designing Binary Encodings for JSON and VARIANT
item.description: In this post, we’ll dig into the internal designs of binary encoding for JSON. There’s been little discussion of the trade-offs these encodings make. We’ll see how even a straightforward binary encoding can materially improve retrieval performance.

item.title: I Suddenly Lost My Enthusiasm for Interneting
item.description: Why the internet feels like total garbage. Why the literacy level of the content available online looks, to me, like it decreases exponentially.
item.author: (https://www.facebook.com/durmonski/)

item.title: I Did My Taxes with Claude. Here's What Happened.
item.description:
It’s tax day — so, shoutout to my tax friends. I hope you get some sleep tonight.
I got mine done. Twice, actually: once with Claude, once with TurboTax, then reconciled the two side-by-side to the dollar. I was curious how AI would handle a workflow we all know — and

item.title: The Permission System
item.description: It is April in Cyprus and the windows are open. I have been sitting with a coffee that went cold twenty minutes ago, thinking about a conversation I had in January last year, on a video call from the desk in my home office in Düsseldorf, with a manager who

item.title: Writing an LLM from scratch, part 32k -- Interventions: training a better model locally with gradient accumulation
item.description: Having worked out which combination of interventions into my model and training run improved loss the most, it was time to see how well that worked with a local run, which meant I had to learn about gradient accumulation
item.author: (Giles Thomas)

item.title: Introduction
item.description: Gym Anything is a framework for building and running computer-use environments.

item.title: One Layer, +12%: What 667 Configs Reveal About Small LLM Anatomy - Austin's Nerdy Things
item.description: I’ve been messing around with local LLMs on my 3090 for a while now — I have a growing collection of Qwen models on D:LLM that I probably should be embarrassed about. A few weeks ago I stumbled across David Noel Ng’s LLM Neuroanatomy blog posts, where he showed that you can take a pretrained […]

item.title: Daniel's Blog · Rules, Not Weights
item.description: Mainstream ML fixes the scoring rule and trains the weights. We're exploring the opposite — searching the rule.

item.title: We Are in This Together
item.description: Harper's mission is to encourage and assist people with communicating their complex ideas and emotions.

item.title: Fun with EML (Exp-Minus-Log)
item.description: A paper is proposing that most elementary functions such as sin, cos, 1/x can be expressed as a combination of the following function and the constant 1. eml(x,y)=exp(x)−ln(y)eml(x,y) = exp(x) &#82…

item.title: My 14-Year Journey Away from ORMs - How I Built pGenie, the SQL-First Postgres Code Generator
item.description: Hi, I’m Nikita Volkov - architect, consultant, and the author of hasql, one of the two main PostgreSQL drivers in Haskell used in major production projects like PostgREST and IHP. After 25 years in IT and more late-night schema-drift fires than I care to count, I open-sourced something I wish had existed a decade ago: pGenie.

item.title: The Great Software Meltdown
item.description: Naval called it. Software was eaten by AI.— Naval (@naval) March 14, 2026 And the market already agreed. Atlassian down 75%. HubSpot down 69%. Figma
item.author: (https://www.facebook.com/Danmartell/)

item.title: I don't care that it's X times faster
item.description: Many new project announcements these days come with a tagline of 'X times faster than ...' and I need to rant about it.

item.title: Things you didn't know about indexes
item.description: Let’s start with things you probably did know.

item.title: 🚂 Temporal — Under the Hood
item.description: What actually happens when you start a Temporal workflow? We trace every SQL statement, count the event-history nodes, and then watch Absurd — a 5-table, single-SQL-file system — do the same job with one-fifth the queries and twenty-times the throughput.

item.title: I Connected a Desktop Phone to a FreeBSD Server, so Now I Can Call It
item.description: I turned a Panasonic KX-T2315 desk phone into a physical menu for my FreeBSD server. When I pick up the handset, the phone adapter calls Asterisk, waits for one digit, and triggers a predefined script on the FreeBSD server. This post covers the phone restoration, HT801 setup, Asterisk configuration, Lua dialplan, Python AGI dispatcher, and the full config download.

item.title: How Wispr Flow Ate My Spacebar: A Forensic Investigation Into Silent Keyboard Interception
item.description: A deep forensic investigation into how Wispr Flow silently intercepts every keystroke via CGEventTap, suppressed 145 spacebar presses, tracks every app and URL, and uploads data even with sharing disabled.
item.author: (Wensen Wu)

item.title: Turning Meshes into Horrifying Piecewise Functions
item.description: I recently wrote a program, mesh2frep, that is, honestly, a bit unhinged at best. Let me tell you about it!
item.author: (https://luke.vuksta.com/about/)
